refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and relevant stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire material throughput data (volume or mass per time interval) during equipment operation.
Calculate and aggregate throughput metrics from acquired data.
Report throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in human-readable format.

candidate_dps: DP1: Continuous volumetric flow measurement via inline sensor with real-time signal conditioning and local data buffering
DP2: Periodic mass-based sampling with calibrated weighing and extrapolation to throughput rate over fixed time windows
DP3: Indirect throughput inference from operational state (runtime, equipment speed, material density) combined with pre-calibrated conversion factors
DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric primary + mass-based verification) with automated cross-validation and anomaly flagging
DP5: Distributed measurement nodes (upstream intake + downstream discharge) with differential accounting to isolate system throughput from external losses
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Hybrid dual-path measurement (volumetric primary + mass-based verification) with automated cross-validation and anomaly flagging

potential_sources: Tight coupling between volumetric and mass measurement calibration schedules—mitigated by independent sensor maintenance windows and asynchronous validation logic
Data synchronization latency between primary and verification paths—acceptable if cross-validation window is ≥ evaluation period granularity
Dependency on material density assumptions for mass-to-volume conversion—decoupled by treating density as a configurable parameter with sensitivity analysis
Reporting module must consume both measurement streams—addressed by defining a clear data contract (schema, timing, error codes) between measurement and reporting subsystems
coupling_check.notes: DP4 avoids single-point-of-failure risk inherent in DP1 or DP2 alone. Volumetric path provides continuous real-time feedback; mass path provides ground truth validation. Coupling is primarily data-flow (not control-flow), enabling independent module development and testing. Cross-validation logic is stateless and can be implemented as a pure function, reducing architectural brittleness.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Material properties (density, particle size distribution) remain stable or are known/measurable during evaluation period. RISK: Significant density variation (e.g., moisture content swings) invalidates mass-to-volume conversion. MITIGATION: Include density measurement or real-time calibration checkpoint.

ASSUMPTION: Both volumetric and mass sensors can be installed without major process disruption. RISK: Retrofit constraints may force compromise on sensor placement or accuracy. MITIGATION: Conduct site survey early; consider non-invasive alternatives (e.g., radar, load cells on discharge chute).

ASSUMPTION: Acceptance criteria 'productivity ≥ productivity_target' is defined with clear baseline and measurement window. RISK: Ambiguity in target definition or evaluation period leads to disputes. MITIGATION: Formalize acceptance criteria in writing before DP realization; define statistical confidence interval.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits installation and operation of measurement equipment without additional guarding or isolation. RISK: Safety constraints may limit sensor accessibility or require redundant safety interlocks. MITIGATION: Engage safety officer early in DP refinement.

ASSUMPTION: Reporting interface (frequency, format, access control) is compatible with existing operational workflows. RISK: Mismatch between measurement cadence and reporting cadence causes data staleness or information overload. MITIGATION: Define reporting SLA (latency, granularity) as part of FR refinement.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required reporting frequency and latency (e.g., 'real-time dashboard update every 5 minutes' vs. 'daily summary report'); (2) Acceptable measurement uncertainty (e.g., '±5% of productivity_target'); (3) Operational conditions under which measurement is valid (e.g., 'steady-state flow only' vs. 'including transient startup/shutdown'); (4) Data retention and archival requirements for audit/compliance. These clarifications will enable tighter DP-to-IM mapping and reduce risk of over-engineering or under-specification. Additionally, confirm whether 'productivity_target' is absolute throughput or relative improvement, as this affects sensor range and calibration strategy.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement validation and anomaly detection subsystem responsible for dual-path measurement orchestration, cross-validation logic, and safety-critical flagging

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives volumetric and mass-based measurement streams from Vehicle sensors

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits anomaly flags and cross-validation failure alerts to safety-critical systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives measurement configuration requests; returns validation status and measurement confidence metrics

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: dual-path validation and anomaly flagging are inherently safety-critical functions. Measurement cross-validation logic is cohesive within safety domain. Minor diffusion: requires telemetry input and tasking coordination, but both are well-defined external interfaces.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns sensor hardware and raw measurement acquisition, not validation logic. Placing cross-validation here couples measurement interpretation tightly to vehicle state, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion: validation logic would need to diffuse to safety and teleoperation layers.

Very poor fit: Teleoperation is operator-interface and command-execution focused, not measurement validation. Placing dual-path validation here creates semantic mismatch and forces safety-critical logic into operator-facing layer. High diffusion: validation would need to propagate to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is likely a top-level orchestration/integration layer. Placing measurement validation at solution level creates overly broad scope and mixes orchestration with domain-specific validation logic. High diffusion: validation details would scatter across subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP4 (hybrid dual-path measurement with cross-validation and anomaly flagging) is fundamentally a safety-critical validation function. Safety Services is the natural owner because: (1) anomaly flagging is a safety alert responsibility, (2) cross-validation logic ensures measurement integrity for safety decisions, (3) the module already owns SafetyAlert interface for critical notifications, and (4) measurement verification is a prerequisite for safe operation, not a Vehicle or Teleoperation concern.
containment_risks: Latency: Safety Services must process dual-path measurements in real-time; if Telemetry interface introduces unacceptable delay, measurement freshness could degrade.
Sensor coupling: If Vehicle sensors are tightly coupled to specific measurement hardware, Safety Services may lack visibility into sensor health/calibration state, weakening validation confidence.

Configuration complexity: Tasking interface must support dynamic reconfiguration of validation thresholds and cross-validation rules; if Tasking protocol is rigid, Safety Services flexibility is constrained.

Measurement source diversity: If Vehicle produces heterogeneous measurement formats (volumetric vs. mass-based), Safety Services must normalize inputs; lack of standardization increases internal complexity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Acceptance criteria require reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period. Safety Services ownership of dual-path measurement validation directly supports this: cross-validation ensures measurement integrity, anomaly flagging prevents false productivity claims, and SafetyAlert interface enables real-time detection of measurement anomalies that would corrupt productivity reporting. Verification is feasible via telemetry logs (volumetric + mass streams), validation decision records, and anomaly flag audit trails.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services owns measurement validation logic (DP4), receives raw measurement streams via Telemetry interface, and emits validation status + confidence metrics via Tasking interface. Vehicle owns sensor hardware and raw acquisition; Safety Services owns interpretation and cross-validation. Boundary is well-defined and non-overlapping.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 (hybrid dual-path measurement with automated cross-validation and anomaly flagging) directly addresses FR requirement to enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time. Dual-path approach provides redundancy and confidence; cross-validation ensures reported values are trustworthy; anomaly flagging prevents propagation of erroneous measurements. DP is complete and logically sound.

diffusion_risks: Latency diffusion: If Telemetry interface introduces unacceptable delay (>100ms assumed for real-time safety), measurement freshness degrades and cross-validation becomes stale. Risk: Safety Services may flag valid measurements as anomalous due to temporal misalignment between volumetric and mass streams. Mitigation: Define and enforce Telemetry SLA for measurement delivery latency.

Sensor health coupling: Vehicle module owns sensor calibration state, health status, and hardware diagnostics. Safety Services lacks direct visibility into sensor degradation, potentially weakening validation confidence. Risk: Cross-validation logic may not account for sensor drift or failure modes. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface to include sensor health metadata (calibration timestamp, confidence bounds, hardware status flags).

Configuration protocol rigidity: Tasking interface must support dynamic reconfiguration of validation thresholds, cross-validation rules, and anomaly detection sensitivity. Risk: If Tasking protocol is static or coarse-grained, Safety Services cannot adapt validation behavior to changing operational conditions (e.g., different material types, environmental factors). Mitigation: Define Tasking protocol to support parameterized validation rule updates with versioning and rollback.

Measurement format heterogeneity: Vehicle may produce volumetric measurements in different units (liters, cubic meters) and mass measurements in different units (kg, metric tons) depending on sensor type. Risk: Safety Services must normalize and reconcile heterogeneous formats; lack of standardization increases internal complexity and error surface. Mitigation: Define canonical measurement format in Telemetry interface contract (e.g., SI units with explicit unit tags).
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score of 0.92 for Safety Services is well-justified. Dual-path validation and anomaly flagging are inherently safety-critical functions; Safety Services is the natural owner. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and well-scoped: Telemetry and Tasking are external interfaces with clear contracts. No evidence of DP4 logic scattered across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or NGHE Solution modules. Ownership boundary is clean and cohesive.

recommended_revisions: Extend Telemetry interface specification to include sensor health metadata: calibration timestamp, confidence bounds, hardware status flags, and measurement freshness timestamp. This reduces sensor coupling risk and strengthens validation confidence.

Define Tasking protocol to support parameterized validation rule updates: allow dynamic reconfiguration of cross-validation thresholds, anomaly detection sensitivity, and measurement normalization rules. Include versioning and audit trail for compliance.

Establish Telemetry SLA: maximum latency for measurement delivery (recommend ≤50ms for real-time safety), measurement synchronization tolerance between volumetric and mass streams (recommend ≤10ms), and data freshness guarantee.

Standardize measurement format in Telemetry interface: define canonical units (SI), explicit unit tags, precision/resolution metadata, and measurement timestamp semantics to reduce heterogeneity and internal complexity in Safety Services.

Document cross-validation algorithm in Safety Services design: specify how volumetric and mass measurements are reconciled, what constitutes an anomaly (e.g., >5% divergence), and how confidence metrics are computed. Link to worksite safety plan and operational policies for threshold justification.

Add measurement source diversity handling: if Vehicle produces heterogeneous sensor types (e.g., ultrasonic volumetric + load-cell mass), document normalization and calibration logic in Safety Services to ensure consistent validation across sensor modalities.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence (0.92 containment, clear allocation, feasible verification). However, the recommended_revisions identify five critical interface and protocol specifications that must be resolved before decomposition can proceed safely. These are not sub-FR derivations but rather design-level clarifications required to make DP4 (hybrid dual-path measurement validation) implementable and verifiable. The high safety criticality of measurement integrity demands that latency SLAs, sensor health metadata, Tasking protocol parameterization, measurement format standardization, and cross-validation algorithm documentation be locked in at this level. Proceeding to sub-FRs without these specifications risks diffusion of validation logic across Safety Services and Vehicle modules, and creates verification ambiguity. Revise DP4 specification and interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking) to incorporate all recommended_revisions, then re-gate before decomposing.

revise.why: DP4 (hybrid dual-path measurement with automated cross-validation and anomaly flagging) is logically sound but operationally under-specified. The recommended_revisions expose four critical DP-level design decisions that must be made explicit: (1) Telemetry interface contract must include sensor health metadata, measurement freshness timestamps, and latency SLA (≤50ms); (2) Tasking interface must support parameterized validation rule updates with versioning; (3) Measurement format must be standardized to SI units with explicit unit tags and precision metadata; (4) Cross-validation algorithm must be documented with anomaly thresholds (e.g., >5% divergence), confidence metric computation, and sensor modality normalization logic. These are not new DPs but rather mandatory elaborations of DP4 that eliminate ambiguity and reduce diffusion risk. Revise DP4 specification document to include: (a) Telemetry interface schema with sensor health fields and SLA; (b) Tasking protocol definition for rule parameterization; (c) Canonical measurement format specification; (d) Cross-validation algorithm pseudocode with threshold justification linked to worksite safety plan. Re-gate DP4 against FR and IM after revision.

assumptions: Telemetry interface latency can be constrained to ≤50ms without architectural redesign of Vehicle module.
Vehicle module can extend sensor telemetry to include health metadata (calibration timestamp, confidence bounds, hardware status) without breaking existing sensor driver contracts.
Tasking interface protocol supports parameterized message payloads for dynamic validation rule updates; if not, Tasking protocol must be redesigned before DP4 implementation.

Cross-validation thresholds (e.g., >5% divergence = anomaly) can be justified by worksite safety plan and operational policies; if not, FR may need refinement to specify acceptable measurement tolerance.

Safety Services module has sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time dual-path validation and anomaly detection without introducing unacceptable latency into the measurement reporting pipeline.
Measurement source diversity (heterogeneous sensor types) is expected in Vehicle module; Safety Services design must account for normalization across sensor modalities.
open_risks: Latency diffusion: Telemetry interface introduces >50ms delay, causing temporal misalignment between volumetric and mass streams and false anomaly flagging. Mitigation: Define and enforce Telemetry SLA with measurement delivery latency guarantee and synchronization tolerance (≤10ms).

Sensor health coupling: Vehicle module owns sensor calibration and health state; Safety Services lacks visibility into sensor degradation. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface to include sensor health metadata (calibration timestamp, confidence bounds, hardware status flags).

Tasking protocol rigidity: If Tasking interface does not support parameterized validation rule updates, Safety Services cannot adapt cross-validation thresholds and anomaly detection sensitivity to changing operational conditions. Mitigation: Define Tasking protocol to support dynamic rule reconfiguration with versioning and audit trail.

Measurement format heterogeneity: Vehicle produces measurements in heterogeneous units (liters vs. cubic meters, kg vs. metric tons). Mitigation: Standardize measurement format in Telemetry interface contract (SI units, explicit unit tags, precision metadata).

Cross-validation algorithm ambiguity: Anomaly detection thresholds and confidence metric computation are not documented. Mitigation: Document cross-validation algorithm in Safety Services design with threshold justification linked to worksite safety plan.

Sensor modality normalization: If Vehicle uses heterogeneous sensor types (ultrasonic volumetric + load-cell mass), Safety Services must normalize and reconcile across modalities. Mitigation: Document sensor modality handling in Safety Services design.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification document: schema, latency SLA (≤50ms), synchronization tolerance (≤10ms), sensor health metadata fields, measurement freshness timestamp semantics.
Tasking interface protocol specification: parameterized message format for validation rule updates, versioning scheme, rollback mechanism, audit trail requirements.
Measurement format standard: canonical units (SI), explicit unit tags, precision/resolution metadata, measurement timestamp semantics, unit conversion rules.

Cross-validation algorithm design document: volumetric and mass measurement reconciliation logic, anomaly threshold definition (e.g., >5% divergence), confidence metric computation formula, sensor modality normalization rules.
Worksite safety plan excerpt: acceptable measurement tolerance, operational conditions (material types, environmental factors), threshold justification for anomaly detection.
Vehicle module sensor inventory: list of sensor types, measurement units, calibration protocols, health status reporting capabilities, driver interface contracts.
Safety Services computational capacity analysis: latency budget for dual-path validation and anomaly detection, throughput requirements for measurement streams, real-time constraints.
